title: China Just Built A Two Brain AI Robot: One Body, Two Minds
summary: China revealed JAKA Pi, a compact humanoid with a split AI brain architecture. Vietnam introduced Dyno and VR-H3 humanoids for homes, security, and industrial work. NVIDIA released the Isaac GR00T robot platform paired with Unitree H2 and Jetson Thor for researchers.
writeup: Three humanoid robotics announcements signal an accelerating global race in 2026: Jaka Robotics (Shanghai) unveiled Pi, a compact 4-ft, 27-DOF platform with a split "fusion brain" separating high-level AI reasoning (cerebrum) from real-time EtherCAT motion control (cerebellum); Vietnam's Vin Group entered through two subsidiaries, Vin Big Dynamics with the service-oriented Dino and Vin Robotics with the industrial VRH3; and NVIDIA introduced an open Isaac GR00T humanoid reference design built on a Unitree H2 body with 75 total degrees of freedom and over 2,000 teraflops of onboard compute. The through-line is that distinct architectural choices, split compute, vertical hardware integration, and open reference platforms, are emerging as the real differentiators as the field moves past proof-of-concept walking demos.
key_points: Jaka Pi's fusion brain architecture runs on Intel's heterogeneous computing platform and separates high-level AI (vision, LLMs, reasoning) from low-level motion control over an EtherCAT network with millisecond-level latency, a design choice driven by the fundamentally different timing requirements of cognition versus coordinated movement.

Pi's integrated joint modules are 15 to 27% smaller than Jaka's previous generation, its knee joints produce 120 Nm (88.5 lb-ft) of torque for stable locomotion, and each arm carries up to 3 kg, making it a research-grade manipulation platform rather than just a walking demo.

Vin Big Dynamics' Dino was field-tested as an autonomous multilingual guide at Vinpearl Safari Phu Quoc, an outdoor deployment with crowds, noise, and lighting changes, which the company cites as evidence of real-world perception and interaction robustness rather than lab-controlled conditions.

Vin Robotics' VRH3 (third-generation) has more than 31 actuators, runs dual onboard edge computers for low-latency autonomous control, handles 6 to 8 kg payloads, and demonstrated VR-headset teleoperation with motion capture built directly into the headset, requiring no external tracking hardware.

Vin Robotics claims full vertical integration of VRH3's mechanical, electrical, power, battery, and AI control systems in-house, a deliberate strategy to control the hardware-software interface across generations rather than integrating third-party components.

NVIDIA's Isaac GR00T reference design pairs a Unitree H2 (31 DOF, roughly 150 lb, nearly 6 ft tall) with dual Sharp Wave tactile five-finger hands (44 additional DOF) for 75 total degrees of freedom, and provides a unified hardware-software foundation so research labs avoid rebuilding simulation, middleware, and sensor integration from scratch.
